hook_text_english: Download our video game Master of Command: Seven Years War
full_text_english:
Download our video game! https://store.steampowered.com/app/2878450/Master_of_Command_Seven_Years_War/
Game Discord: https://discord.com/invite/SDxBahCGG8

Channel Discord: https://discord.gg/thearmchairhistorian
Twitter: https://twitter.com/ArmchairHist
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/armchairhistorianshow

Sources:

Alexander, Joseph H. Across the Reef: The Marine Assault of Tarawa. Washington, D.C.: Marine Corps Historical Center, 1993.

Ballendorf, Dirk. “Earl Hanock Ellis: The Man and His Mission.” Proceedings US Naval Institute, 969, 109, no. 11 (1983). https://www.usni.org/magazines/proceedings/1983/november/earl-hanock-ellis-man-and-his-mission

Borneman, Walter R. The Admirals: Nimitz, Halsey, Leahy, and King-- the five-star admirals who won the war at sea. New York, NY: Back Bay Books, Little Brown and Company, 2013. 

“Prelude to War.” Naval History and Heritage Command, 2021. https://www.history.navy.mil/browse-by-topic/wars-conflicts-and-operations/world-war-ii/1941/prelude.html

Sledge, Eugene. With the Old Breed: At Peleliu and Okinawa. New York: Presidio Press, 2007. 

Stevenson, W.D. "The Higgins Boat (LCVP) the Boat that Won WWII," FRA Today, (August 2019): 16-22.

Toll, I.W. The Conquering Tide: War in the Pacific Islands, 1942-1944. New York, NY: W.W. Norton & Company, 2016.

The National WWII Museum. "The Pacific Strategy, 1941-1944" Published April 13, 2025. Accessed April 13, 2025. https://www.nationalww2museum.org/war/articles/pacific-strategy-1941-1944.


Armchair Team Credits:
https://docs.google.com/document/d/1sYjrtdKP67bvEH4UWOv-24_dwJGxZ395ZuczyKOUFNw/edit?usp=sharing
body_summary_english: Promotes video game download on Steam with links to game and channel Discord communities.
comments:
Download our video game! https://store.steampowered.com/app/2878450/Master_of_Command_Seven_Years_War/
Game Discord: https://discord.com/invite/SDxBahCGG8

Channel Discord: https://discord.gg/thearmchairhistorian
Twitter: https://twitter.com/ArmchairHist
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/armchairhistorianshow
We will miss you.
The armchair historian definitely will be missed...

I don't wanna see the end of this ever...
We'll miss yah buddy
This is as marvelous as always but I'm in agony for you leaving. We will all miss you but know this! We will always support you, Griffin. ❤

My grandpa fought on Tarawa. He told me he was in the 2nd wave. He had to direct the amphibious tractors by waving his shirt. He figured later that the Japanese didn't fire at him because they thought he was crazy. He told me "they" had to throw satchel charges into the pillboxes so I don't know if he did it himself. Was surprised there wasn't a bonzai charge the 1st night. I miss my Gunny.
Every time I see a new video, I’m happy before I realise that every video is one closer to the last…
11:03 Nice World at War reference
goodbye to a legend
we'll miss you
You have played your part well in this theatre, we applaud as you exit
I love the reference of world at war in here that is still one of my favorite games to play

"We will miss you" comments: 99%
Video comments: 1%
We are witnessing the passing of an age my friends... The armchair historian will be missed.
And Griffin we will always miss you and continue to support your content
We’ll remember you
RIP armchair:(
Both you and your historical content will be missed, this is my favorite Channel on YouTube by far
we'll miss u, ur the one who make me realize that history is not boring
He will be missed
we'll miss you Griffin, but we'll always support you.
the armchair historian  will be passed trough history. goodbye legend.
We'll miss you
We’ll miss you!!
Just saying my thanks for years of great, high quality content! Love the channel and I hope you all are proud of what you achieved
Good night, sir.
You gave hours of enjoyment to many... you will be missed
we will miss you, comrade🫡

And I'll take with me the memories
To be my sunshine after the rain
It's so hard to say goodbye to yesterday
I will miss you
I will miss you bro 💔
This is still one of the best YouTube channels
No body can or will ever replace you in our hearts 😢
Thank you for having your most spectacular videos in your final year❤. You will definitely be missed 😢

We will all miss your incredible History content. I honestly find your content to be so enjoyable and informative so it breaks my heart that you will be leaving. You have my deepest respect for all the hours for teaching us so much about historic figures, events and perspectives. You will always be missed
How dare the algorithm hide me from this for 28 seconds
I’m so sad, this has always been my favorite channel 😢
Every upload is a blessing
We will miss you
We all love you and we will all remember.You words cannot describe how much we will all miss you
title: U.S. Island Hopping Strategy
caption:
Download our video game! https://store.steampowered.com/app/2878450/Master_of_Command_Seven_Years_War/
Game Discord: https://discord.com/invite/SDxBahCGG8

Channel Discord: https://discord.gg/thearmchairhistorian
Twitter: https://twitter.com/ArmchairHist
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/armchairhistorianshow

Sources:

Alexander, Joseph H. Across the Reef: The Marine Assault of Tarawa. Washington, D.C.: Marine Corps Historical Center, 1993.

Ballendorf, Dirk. “Earl Hanock Ellis: The Man and His Mission.” Proceedings US Naval Institute, 969, 109, no. 11 (1983). https://www.usni.org/magazines/proceedings/1983/november/earl-hanock-ellis-man-and-his-mission

Borneman, Walter R. The Admirals: Nimitz, Halsey, Leahy, and King-- the five-star admirals who won the war at sea. New York, NY: Back Bay Books, Little Brown and Company, 2013. 

“Prelude to War.” Naval History and Heritage Command, 2021. https://www.history.navy.mil/browse-by-topic/wars-conflicts-and-operations/world-war-ii/1941/prelude.html

Sledge, Eugene. With the Old Breed: At Peleliu and Okinawa. New York: Presidio Press, 2007. 

Stevenson, W.D. "The Higgins Boat (LCVP) the Boat that Won WWII," FRA Today, (August 2019): 16-22.

Toll, I.W. The Conquering Tide: War in the Pacific Islands, 1942-1944. New York, NY: W.W. Norton & Company, 2016.

The National WWII Museum. "The Pacific Strategy, 1941-1944" Published April 13, 2025. Accessed April 13, 2025. https://www.nationalww2museum.org/war/articles/pacific-strategy-1941-1944.


Armchair Team Credits:
https://docs.google.com/document/d/1sYjrtdKP67bvEH4UWOv-24_dwJGxZ395ZuczyKOUFNw/edit?usp=sharing
transcript: Waves of US Marines packed the landing zones on Betio Island, pushing forward step by step. They advance into a hail of bullets, trapped in the deadly crossfire from entrenched Japanese positions. Though they vastly outnumber the Japanese Special Landing Forces defending the island, US forces face 76 hours of hellish combat. Obstacles and pillboxes cluttered the island where 1,500 Americans lie dead on the first day. The bloody morning of Tarawa marks the first contested landing of the island-hopping campaign against the Japanese Empire. Long before American forces waded ashore on Pacific atolls, US military planners had anticipated a potential conflict in the region. Starting in 1906, the US Naval War College and the Joint Planning Committee developed a series of plans for hostilities against the rising Japanese Empire, code-named War Plan Orange. The plan envisioned a decisive naval battle near the Philippines if the Japanese Navy struck this US-held territory. American battleships and cruisers would deploy from their naval bases in the Pacific to rescue their outnumbered allies, and together they would pound the Japanese fleet into submission. The Joint Planning Committee revised War Plan Orange nine times before ultimately scrapping it in the 1930s. Both the Army and the Navy's thinking evolved in response to new strategic realities. For one, Orange assumed US naval superiority was a given, yet the Imperial Japanese Navy gained valuable experience during the First World War and expanded significantly after renouncing the Washington Naval Treaty. The Japanese seizure of German [music] colonies in the South Pacific created a potential barrier to US shipping lanes to the Philippines. Japanese warships could concentrate their forces and fight US fleets before they could be reinforced from their far-flung territories. Meanwhile, advances in weapon systems challenged the supremacy of heavily armed battleship formations. Submarines could stalk and US squadrons over long distances. Even heavily [music] armored warships could fall prey to repeated aerial attacks without air support. Officers in the US Marine Corps proposed an incremental campaign across the Pacific rather than a single climactic battle. In 1923 operations plan, Lieutenant Colonel Earl Hancock Ellis advocated [music] for rapid amphibious landings supported by naval bombardment and aircraft providing cover [music] to seize enemy shores. He noted that specific island chains were to [music] be secured and estimated the manpower and equipment requirements to accomplish the operations. Once captured, these islands would serve as forward bases from which the Navy could launch offensive operations deeper into [music] Japanese held territory. December 7th, 1941 shattered any remaining illusions about a single decisive battle deciding the war. The Philippines fell soon after and US Army General Douglas MacArthur was forced to turn tail. Japanese task forces soon captured other American territories like Guam and Wake Island along with hundreds of POWs. The Japanese Empire expanded rapidly into the South and West, even threatening an invasion of Australia. However, by late 1942, US naval and air forces dealt heavy blows to the Imperial Japanese Navy during the battles of Midway and the Coral Sea. Forced to recoup its losses, Japanese expansion ground to a halt. Although the United States now had an opportunity to strike back and the American public was eager to take the war to Japan, President Franklin D. Roosevelt's promise to Churchill to prioritize the European theater delayed the Pacific offensive. A complex set of problems stood in the way of the US Navy and Army. The first was distance. The Pacific theater was enormous, many times larger than the European theater. The US and Japan conducted a war in an area that spanned tens of millions of square kilometers. Distance meant difficulty locating enemy fleets or aircraft, time and fuel spent traveling, and challenges to supp
